file_name: You et al. - 2023 - Integration of cellular-resolution optical coherence tomography and Raman spectroscopy for discrimin.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning algorithms, specifically Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Decision Tree (TREE), to classify five types of skin cells (keratinocytes, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytes, and melanoma) using features extracted from cellular-resolution OCT images and Raman spectra.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH PAPER' in the header. It describes original experimental work involving the development of an integrated OCT and Raman spectroscopy system, the acquisition of data from five types of skin cells, and the application of machine learning algorithms to classify these cells, resulting in new accuracy metrics.

title: Integration of cellular-resolution optical coherence tomography and Raman spectroscopy for discrimination of skin cancer cells with machine learning
brief_summary: This study developed an integrated cellular-resolution optical coherence tomography (OCT) and near-infrared Raman spectroscopy system to discriminate between five types of skin cells, including normal keratinocytes, melanocytes, and cancerous squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma cells. The researchers extracted 3D morphological features from OCT images and full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1) after removing fluorescence components. Machine learning algorithms, specifically Linear Discriminant Analysis, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Decision Trees, were employed to classify these cell types based on the extracted features. The results demonstrated that while OCT features effectively differentiated cancerous from normal cells with 85% accuracy, Raman spectroscopy alone achieved nearly 100% accuracy in distinguishing cancer cell lines. The study concludes that integrating both modalities leverages their complementary strengths, with OCT excelling in morphological differentiation and Raman spectroscopy providing high-accuracy chemical composition-based classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Cancer cell line used in experiments; part of keratinocyte-based cells category.

all_task_types.target: Five types of skin cells (keratinocytes, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytes, and melanoma) and binary distinctions (cancer vs. normal, keratinocyte vs. melanocyte).
all_task_types.details: The paper employs machine learning algorithms (Linear Discriminant Analysis, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Decision Tree) to classify discrete diagnostic categories of skin cells. The task involves multiclass classification among five cell types and binary classification tasks (e.g., cancerous versus normal cells). Accuracy metrics (85% for five types, nearly 100% for Raman-based cancer discrimination) are reported as the primary outcome, indicating a classification task rather than regression.

datasets_used.name: In vitro skin cell dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom experimental dataset consisting of five types of in vitro skin cells measured using an integrated cellular-resolution optical coherence tomography (OCT) and near-infrared Raman spectroscopy system. The cells were prepared by Ms. Ming-Yi Lin and Dr. Jeng-Wei Tjiu. Data includes 3D OCT images (1166.4 μm × 878.4 μm × 200 μm) and Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1).

use.training: Used for training machine learning algorithms (Linear Discriminant Analysis, K-Nearest Neighbors, Decision Tree) to classify cell types based on extracted 3D OCT features and full Raman spectra.
use.testing: Used for testing classification accuracy of the machine learning models.
use.other: The dataset was used to evaluate the complementary strengths of OCT (morphological differentiation) and Raman spectroscopy (chemical composition-based classification).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of machine learning algorithms (LDA, KNN, TREE) by splitting the dataset into training and testing sets for each fold.
To assess the statistical significance of differences in features (e.g., cell volume, compactness, surface roughness) between cell types, using thresholds of p < 0.001, p < 0.05, and p > 0.05.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance testing (implied t-test or ANOVA based on p-value reporting for feature comparison)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the measured feature (e.g., cell volume, compactness, surface roughness, average intensity, internal intensity standard deviation) between the compared cell types.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the measured feature between the compared cell types.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Significance was determined using p-value thresholds: p < 0.001 for significant difference, p < 0.05 for slight difference, and p > 0.05 for almost no difference. These thresholds were used to evaluate the effectiveness of OCT features in differentiating between cancerous and normal cells, as well as between keratinocyte and melanocyte cells.

experiment_1_5_class_classification.notes: Classifying five types of skin cells (keratinocytes, squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytes, and melanoma). The paper states an accuracy of 85% was achieved. Specific breakdown per model for this specific 5-class task is not explicitly detailed in the text, but the ensemble approach using LDA, KNN, and TREE is described.

experiment_2_cancer_vs_normal_OCT.notes: Differentiating cancer cells from normal cells using 3D OCT image features. The decision tree algorithm achieved 85.9% accuracy. Mean intracellular intensity and standard deviation of intensity also achieved >85% accuracy.

experiment_3_keratinocyte_vs_melanocyte_.notes: Distinguishing normal keratinocytes (HaCaT) from melanocytes. The paper notes 'good discrimination results' and 'quite high' accuracy, attributing this to morphological differences in cell size and shape. No specific numerical value is provided for this binary classification.

experiment_4_cancer_vs_cancer_OCT.notes: Classifying among cancer cells (melanoma vs keratinocyte-based skin cancer cell lines; BCC vs SCC). The classification ability of OCT images is relatively low, reaching nearly 70% accuracy.

experiment_5_melanoma_vs_keratinocyte_ca.notes: Discriminating melanoma (A375) versus keratinocyte-based skin cancer cell lines (BCC & SCC) using full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1).

experiment_6_BCC_vs_SCC_Raman.notes: Discriminating Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1).

experiment_7_all_cancer_cells_Raman.notes: Discriminating all three cancer cell lines using Raman spectra. The paper states the classification accuracy is near 100%.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Decision Tree (TREE)
architecture_used.input_size: 3D OCT features (cell volume, compactness, surface roughness, average intensity, internal intensity standard deviation) and full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Class labels (5 skin cell types or binary distinctions)

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering in the horizontal plane (x-y plane) and depth direction (z-axis) to match system spatial resolutions (0.45 μm in x-y, 0.2 μm in z; filter radii selected as 2 pixels horizontally and 4.5 pixels vertically).

Binarization process including threshold setting (using ImageJ’s auto threshold function), watershed algorithm for segmenting cell clusters and debris, and erosion operations (3x3 pixel structural element) to retain cell images.
Removal of fluorescence components from acquired Raman spectra.
Feature extraction from 3D OCT images: cell volume, compactness, surface roughness, average intensity, and internal intensity standard deviation.
Use of full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1) as input features.
conclusions: Cell surface roughness, internal average intensity, and standard deviation of internal intensity distribution effectively differentiate cancerous and normal cells in OCT image features.
The three OCT features (surface roughness, internal average intensity, and standard deviation of internal intensity) also work well in sorting keratinocytes and melanocytes.
Using full Raman spectra (600 to 2100 cm−1), melanoma and keratinocyte-based cell carcinoma cancer cells can be discriminated effectively.
All three cancer cell lines can be discriminated using Raman spectra with classification accuracy near 100%.
OCT is effective for distinguishing between normal cell lines and between normal and cancer cell lines, while Raman spectroscopy is suitable for discriminating among different cancer cell lines.
The classification ability of OCT images is relatively low for distinguishing among cancer cells (reaching nearly 70% accuracy) due to lack of significant morphological differences among them.
Raman spectral feature calculation time is 3 to 10 times longer than 3D OCT image feature calculation time (6 to 50 seconds vs. 2 to 5 seconds).
The advantages and disadvantages of OCT 3D image features and Raman spectra are complementary, and integrating both can help improve the differentiation between skin cancer and normal cell lines.
Integrating cellular-resolution OCT with near-infrared Raman spectroscopy can serve as an important diagnostic tool and direction for future clinical studies.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on in vitro cell lines (HaCaT, A431, BCC-1/KMC, primary melanocytes, A375) rather than in vivo tissue samples or clinical patient data, limiting immediate clinical generalizability.
The total dataset size is small (283 cells total), which may lead to overfitting or biased performance estimates despite the use of 10-fold cross-validation.
No external validation was performed; the models were only tested on the same in vitro dataset used for training.
OCT-based classification among different cancer types (e.g., BCC vs. SCC) showed relatively low accuracy (~70%), attributed to a lack of significant morphological differences among cancer cells.
Raman spectroscopy feature calculation is significantly slower (6 to 50 seconds) compared to OCT feature calculation (2 to 5 seconds), which may impact real-time deployment feasibility.
The standard deviation of Raman spectra for normal cells was found to be two to five times that of cancer cells, which negatively impacts the accuracy of normal cell classification using Raman alone.
Data availability is restricted to 'upon reasonable request,' which hinders reproducibility and independent verification of the results.
The paper does not report confidence intervals for the accuracy metrics, only point estimates (e.g., 85%, near 100%).

The study acknowledges that quantifying morphology and intensity via OCT alone cannot perfectly distinguish cells, particularly between cancer types, necessitating the integration with Raman spectroscopy.
